title: Exploratory factor analysis on numeric data
summary: Rust port of psych::fa. For each batch defined in meta, fit one factor model per n_factors in [min_count, max_count] and emit a single .xlsx workbook. Each batch gets two sheets — `<batch>_sum` (eigenvalues across k) and `<batch>_load` (per-variable loadings across k) — with results for every k stacked vertically under a shared header.
description: Pipeline per (batch, k): build the correlation matrix with the selected `cor` method, extract k factors with the selected `fm` method (SMC communality init; fallback to max-abs-off-diag on singular R), rotate with the selected `rotate` method, and record eigenvalues, per-variable loadings MR1..MRk, communality h², uniqueness u², Hoffman complexity, and dominant factor index. Loading rows within a sheet are sorted by dominant factor ascending then |loading| descending (matching psych's fa.sort).
usage:
[["factor-analysis::fa"]]
input = "./data.sav"
run_dir = "./runs"
run_name = "fa1"
min_count = 2
max_count = 5
cor = "pearson"
fm = "minres"
rotate = "varimax"

meta = [
  { match = "^att_", batch = "attitudes", type = "ordinal" },
  { match = "^sat_(.+)", batch = "satisfaction", label_template = "Satisfaction: ${1}" },
  { variable = "nps", batch = "outcomes", label = "Net Promoter Score" },
]

arguments.name: input
arguments.description: Path to the input file. `.csv` / `.tsv` use the CSV reader; `.sav` / `.zsav` use the SPSS reader.

arguments.name: meta
arguments.description: Variable metadata: either a path to a meta CSV, or an inline TOML list of row (`variable = ...`) and pattern (`match = ...`) entries. See the Inline meta notes below.

arguments.name: run_dir
arguments.description: Base directory for outputs. Files land in `<run_dir>/ocinfo/<run_name>_factor_analysis/`.

arguments.name: run_name
arguments.description: Short identifier for this run; no `..`, `/`, or `\`.

arguments.name: batches
arguments.description: Subset of batch names to analyse. Empty = all batches from meta (or a single `all` batch if meta has no batch column).

arguments.name: min_count
arguments.description: Smallest n_factors to extract per batch.

arguments.name: max_count
arguments.description: Largest n_factors to extract per batch. Clamped to (n_vars - 1).

arguments.name: cor
arguments.description: Correlation method used to build R. See the Correlation methods note for the full list.

arguments.name: fm
arguments.description: Factoring method used to extract loadings. See the Factoring methods note for the full list.

arguments.name: rotate
arguments.description: Rotation applied to the loading matrix. See the Rotation methods note for the full list.

arguments.name: correct
arguments.description: Continuity correction for zero cells in tetrachoric/polychoric contingency tables (matches psych defaults).

arguments.name: na_action
arguments.description: `complete_cases` (pairwise deletion per correlation cell) or `fail` (abort on NA).

arguments.name: headers
arguments.description: Whether CSV inputs have a header row. Ignored for `.sav` inputs.

arguments.name: threads
arguments.description: Worker thread count. `1` runs serially. Results are deterministic regardless of thread count.

examples.title: CSV with external meta CSV
examples.body:
[["factor-analysis::fa"]]
input = "./data.csv"
meta = "./meta.csv"
run_dir = "./runs"
run_name = "fa1"
min_count = 2
max_count = 5
cor = "pearson"
rotate = "varimax"

examples.title: Dichotomous items with tetrachoric correlations
examples.body:
[["factor-analysis::fa"]]
input = "./items.sav"
run_dir = "./runs"
run_name = "tetra"
cor = "tetrachoric"
rotate = "varimax"

# type = dichotomous is required for tetrachoric correlations.
meta = [
  { match = "^P1_", batch = "attitudes", type = "dichotomous" },
]

examples.title: Output: <batch>_sum sheet
examples.body:
One row per (n_factors, eigenvalue dim). Header cells:
  batch, cor, fm, rotate, n_obs  -- shared metadata block
  n_factors | iterations | converged | dim | eigenvalue | diff | prop | cumulative

examples.title: Output: <batch>_load sheet
examples.body:
One row per (n_factors, variable). MR columns widen to the largest k in the batch; rows with smaller k leave higher MR columns blank.
  n_factors | variable | label | MR1 .. MR<max_k> | h2 | u2 | com | dominant

notes: Inline meta semantics: entries are evaluated in order, all-matches-apply. A column hit by multiple pattern entries is added once per entry, so one column can belong to multiple batches. Unmatched columns are silently dropped. Row entries use `variable = "..."`; pattern entries use `match = "<regex>"` (Rust regex crate; no backrefs or lookaround).

Label templates (pattern entries): `label_template = "Satisfaction: ${1}"` expands `${name}` (whole column name), `${0}` (whole regex match), and `${1}`, `${2}`, ... (positional captures). Omitted template → SPSS variable label (`.sav`) or column name (CSV).

SPSS inputs: user-defined missing values coerce to NA on read. Empty meta fields fill in from SPSS metadata — label ← variable label; type ← value-labels present → ordinal, plain numeric → numeric, string → categorical.
Correlation methods (cor): pearson, covariance, tetrachoric, polychoric, mixed, biserial, polyserial, yuleb, yuleq, yuley. All implemented (Phases 1–2).

Factoring methods (fm): minres, pa, uls, ols, wls, gls, ml, minchi, minrank, alpha. Currently minres and pa are implemented (both route to iterative principal-axis); other values validate but return NotImplemented at runtime (Phase 3).

Rotation methods (rotate): none, varimax, quartimax, bentler_t, equamax, varimin, geomin_t, bifactor, promax, oblimin, simplimax, bentler_q, geomin_q, biquartimin, cluster. Currently none and varimax are implemented (Kaiser-normalised analytic Varimax matching stats::varimax); other values validate but return NotImplemented at runtime (Phase 3).

Type vs. cor compatibility: tetrachoric requires dichotomous columns, polychoric requires ordinal (or dichotomous), biserial/polyserial mix numeric with the categorical partner. If meta leaves type blank the default is numeric, which will cause those correlation methods to fail with an explicit per-pair error.
Sample size per batch is the minimum non-zero n_obs across all pairs in R — a conservative choice matching psych::fa's default pairwise behaviour.
`com` is Hoffman's complexity: 1.0 means a variable loads on one factor; values approach n_factors as loadings are spread evenly.
